questions.question: What is the unit of resistance in the International System of Units (SI)?
questions.explanation: The Ohm is the SI unit of electrical resistance, defined as the resistance between two points on a conductor when a constant potential difference of 1 volt exists between them.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating the total resistance in a series circuit?
questions.explanation: The total resistance in a series circuit is the sum of all individual resistances.

questions.question: What is the unit of power in the International System of Units (SI)?
questions.explanation: The Watt is the SI unit of power, defined as one joule per second.

questions.question: What is Ohm's Law?
questions.explanation: Ohm's Law states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage applied across it, and inversely proportional to its resistance.

questions.question: What is the difference between a conductor and an insulator?
questions.correct_answer: Conductors allow the flow of electric current, while insulators do not.
questions.explanation: Materials that allow electric current to flow are called conductors, whereas materials that prevent the flow of electric current are called insulators.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a fuse in an electrical circuit?
questions.correct_answer: To protect against excessive current flow
questions.explanation: A fuse is a safety device that melts and breaks the circuit when an excessive current flows, preventing damage to the circuit.

questions.question: What is the definition of electric potential difference?
questions.correct_answer: The work required to move a unit charge between two points
questions.explanation: Electric potential difference is the work required to move a unit charge between two points, and it is measured in volts.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating the current flowing through a conductor?
questions.explanation: The current flowing through a conductor can be calculated using Ohm's Law, which states that I = V/R.

questions.question: What is the difference between a series circuit and a parallel circuit?
questions.correct_answer: In a series circuit, components are connected end-to-end, while in a parallel circuit, components are connected between the same two points
questions.explanation: Series circuits have one path for current flow, whereas parallel circuits have multiple paths.

questions.question: What is the unit of electric charge in the International System of Units (SI)?
questions.explanation: The Coulomb is the SI unit of electric charge, defined as the amount of charge that flows through a conductor when a potential difference of 1 volt exists between two points.

questions.question: What is the definition of electric current?
questions.explanation: Electric current is defined as the flow of electric charge through a conductor.

notes.title: Ohm's Law
notes.content: V = IR, where V is voltage, I is current, and R is resistance.

notes.title: Series Circuit
notes.content: Components are connected end-to-end in a series circuit.

notes.title: Parallel Circuit
notes.content: Components are connected between the same two points in a parallel circuit.

notes.title: Conductor
notes.content: Materials that allow electric current to flow are called conductors.

notes.title: Insulator
notes.content: Materials that prevent the flow of electric current are called insulators.

notes.title: Fuse
notes.content: A fuse is a safety device that melts and breaks the circuit when an excessive current flows, preventing damage to the circuit.

notes.title: Electric Potential Difference
notes.content: The work required to move a unit charge between two points.

notes.title: Current Flowing Through a Conductor
notes.content: I = V/R, where I is current, V is voltage, and R is resistance.

notes.title: Resistance in Series Circuit
notes.content: Rt = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn, where Rt is total resistance, and Ri are individual resistances.

notes.title: Electric Charge
notes.content: The amount of charge that flows through a conductor when a potential difference exists between two points.

facts.fact: Ohm's Law states that V = IR, where V is voltage, I is current, and R is resistance.
The total resistance in a series circuit is the sum of all individual resistances.
The unit of power in the International System of Units (SI) is the Watt.
Electric potential difference is measured in volts.
The unit of electric charge in the International System of Units (SI) is the Coulomb.
A fuse is a safety device that melts and breaks the circuit when an excessive current flows, preventing damage to the circuit.
The flow of electric charge through a conductor is defined as electric current.
Components are connected end-to-end in a series circuit, and between the same two points in a parallel circuit.
Materials that allow electric current to flow are called conductors, while materials that prevent the flow of electric current are called insulators.
The formula for calculating the total resistance in a series circuit is Rt = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn.
